chapters.title: Upgrade, downgrade, or rebuild from scratch
chapters.text:
Do you remember floppy disks and cassette tapes? They show how digital change wiped out entire product lines and left once-hot trends behind.

There are several reasons a company decides to rework its business model, the blueprint for how a firm creates value, brings it to market, and earns a profit. A company may need to respond to a previous model that failed, improve on the current one, hunt for the best setup for a new project, or prepare for an uncertain market.

As a result, the average lifespan of a business model has shrunk sharply over the past fifty years. The Boston Consulting Group estimates that a model stays effective for about five years.

If you stop innovating, inertia pulls you backward.

So how do you design smart models that keep your business alive? Stick with this summary to find a tested roadmap used by IBM and Deloitte. Your business transformation can start right now.

chapters.title: The building blocks of innovation
chapters.text:
Building a new business model starts with mobilizing, which means recognizing why change is needed, telling colleagues about it, and pulling a team together to work on it. The next step is understanding, since you should learn about existing models before creating something new. Here are the pieces that make up the business model canvas.

Customer segments are the groups you choose to serve, sorted by shared traits such as needs or by the channels that reach them, like the internet, retailers, or wholesalers.

Value propositions do not have to be brand new, but they need qualities that make them stand out. Price, design, customization, performance, accessibility, or risk reduction are common levers.

Channels cover every way you reach customers at each stage, from building awareness to post-sale support. The trick is balancing cost-effective channels with the ones customers prefer, and balancing owned channels with partner channels, since owned ones bring more revenue and partner ones expand your reach.

Revenue streams rely on knowing what your target audience will pay for. Some prefer to buy, others prefer to rent. Offering both can lift total profit.

Customer relationships come from personal help or automated help, and each leaves a different impression. Plan in advance how you will communicate with customers.

Key resources are the mix of human, intellectual, physical, and financial assets that a particular model needs to thrive.

Key activities are the tasks your company should prioritize, such as production, knowledge management, or platform promotion.

Key partnerships come from deciding whether to outsource a task or share infrastructure with another organization.

Cost structure is the money a business spends on operations, and it depends on the model. Cost-driven companies like Ryanair revolve around low prices and must economize. Other models offer more flexibility.

Take Apple as an example. Its early music model looked simple, since selling iPods drove most of the profit. There was a twist. After negotiating with major record labels, Apple created a fresh value proposition, an easy, accessible, enjoyable way to listen to music. That made the company hard to beat.

Identify the main allies of your company. No business is an island.

chapters.title: Steal ideas from the best in the business
chapters.text:
Let's look at common business patterns to see how companies put the model's building blocks to work.

The first pattern is unbundling. Many companies run several distinct kinds of business under one roof, following a bundled model. Banks, for instance, serve customers, keep platforms running, and create new services, all at once.

Yet these different kinds of business have very different priorities. Customer relationships require a customer-focused approach. Infrastructure business needs scale and cost control. Product innovation needs speed and engaged employees.

As a result, a bundled model can create conflicts of interest. A financial advisor, for example, may be pushed to sell the bank's new products, which can damage the long-term relationship with clients. That is why many companies today prefer an unbundled model. Mobile telecom firms are a good example. They focus on customer relationships and outsource the rest.

The second pattern is the long tail. Selling many niche products in small volumes can pay off well. The trick is to build a strong platform and find a field with low inventory costs. Netflix did this by buying licenses on many films that were not blockbusters. Each film has a limited audience, but together they bring in solid revenue.

The third pattern is the multi-sided platform. Companies can make money by connecting different but interdependent groups of people, such as gamers and game developers. This model is risky because both groups have to be on the platform at the same time. A common way to handle that is to subsidize one side. Some businesses, like Metro newspaper, are free to readers and earn from advertisers. Others, like Microsoft, subsidize developers and earn from customers.

The fourth pattern is FREE. There are three ways to give something at no cost and still earn revenue. The advertising-based model is common in media like Metro and in search engines. The freemium model is a hybrid. Users share photos for free on Flickr but pay for premium options such as unlimited storage. The bait-and-hook model gives something away cheaply or free to lay the groundwork for regular purchases later. Gillette did this with its razor handles and earned the real money on disposable blades.

The fifth pattern is the open business model. Its cornerstone is collaboration with other organizations. A company can, for instance, involve university researchers to solve its problems, which is outside-in innovation, or it can sell unused intellectual property from its research and development department, which is inside-out innovation.

Instead of pouring large sums into innovation, you can buy intellectual property from others.

chapters.title: Get to know your market
chapters.text:
Now that you have a feel for several business strategies, you are ready to design your own model, and an environment analysis is a useful place to start. You can group the outside factors that affect your company into four categories.

Market forces are the first category, and an effective model design starts with a market analysis. Look at needs and demands to see if there are unmet needs you can satisfy and which demands are growing. Check switching costs to see how easily customers can get the same product or service from your rivals, and ask what keeps them with you. Look at market segments to see which customer groups are expanding. Measure revenue attractiveness to see what will bring the highest profit. And track market dynamics to see which key shifts are underway.

Think of environment analysis as outlining your design space. You are identifying your opportunities and your constraints.

Industry forces are the second category, and once you have analyzed the market, you need to learn about the key players. Look at competitors and new entrants to understand their strengths and weaknesses. Look at substitutes to see if companies from other fields compete with your offer. Look at suppliers to see how much your business depends on outside contractors. Look at stakeholders to see who shapes your field, and decide whether lobbying matters.

Key trends are the third category, and since business is a long game, thinking a few moves ahead is essential. Trends come in a few types. Technological trends include innovations that could lift or hurt your value propositions. Regulatory trends cover the impact of government on your market. Societal and socioeconomic trends cover what shapes customer behavior and how that could change.

Macroeconomic forces are the fourth category, and since every company is part of a much larger economy, your analysis should include that view. General conditions include whether you are entering the market at a boom or a downturn. Capital markets include how easy it is to raise money and at what cost. Resources include how price swings can hit your business. Infrastructure includes whether distribution will be smooth and quick.

A useful extra tool for testing a new model is the SWOT analysis applied to each component of the business. It helps you assess your current state by spotting the strengths and weaknesses of each building block, and it helps you look ahead by weighing opportunities and threats.

Alexander Osterwalder and Yves Pigneur once wrote that a competitive business model that works today can be outdated or even obsolete tomorrow.

Alexander Osterwalder,

chapters.title: A toolkit for designing a business
chapters.text:
Designing a new model is a long process full of brainstorming, experimenting, and refining. The following tools can make the designer's work easier.

The first tool is customer insights. Strong research goes past what customers say they want and looks at unmet needs, obstacles, and aspirations. A good way to start is with an empathy map from XPLANE, which explores customers' environments, influences, concerns, conflicts, risks, and expectations.

The second tool is ideation. Companies usually draw ideas for innovation from these parts of the business model canvas. From resources, ask how you can use what you have in a new way. From value propositions, ask what else you can offer. From customers, ask how you can make services more convenient. From revenue streams, ask which tools could bring in new sources of income.

The third tool is visual thinking. Visual tools reveal connections between issues and let you look at tasks from different angles.

Brainstorming with colleagues of different seniority, experience, and background is the most effective approach.

The fourth tool is prototyping. Experimenting with different variables of your business is an endless source of fresh ideas. Questions like, what happens if we cut this revenue stream, pull teams in more than ordinary meetings do and often lead to new opportunities.

Prototyping pairs well with another tool, the four-actions framework, created by the authors of Blue Ocean Strategy. To use it, identify the factors you can eliminate, raise to a higher level, downgrade, or introduce.

The fifth tool is storytelling. Pushback is almost certain when you offer a new model to customers, investors, or colleagues. A tool that lowers skepticism and lets people listen is therefore essential. Storytelling helps you describe new models from an employee's or customer's perspective and highlight the benefits of change.

The sixth tool is scenarios. You can use it in workshops that follow these steps. Market specialists develop scenarios based on environment and SWOT analysis. Colleagues break into groups and discuss which business model fits each scenario best.

That is how a company prepares for the future.

Roger Martin, Dean of the Rotman School of Management, once wrote that businesspeople do not just need to understand designers better. They need to become designers.

Alexander Osterwalder,

chapters.title: From a written plan to real-world results
chapters.text:
Once you have picked a model that fits, you can turn it into a plan with a budget and clear milestones. A few factors drive a successful rollout. You need to predict and manage risks, find a sponsor, and explain to employees why the new model matters.

A special strategy is needed when you add a new business model on top of an existing one, since the two can fight each other or even undermine each other. The Dutch financial group ING, for example, launched the marketing initiative ING Direct. With its online and telephone services, the unit grew quickly and almost overshadowed ING itself.

How can you run different business models productively in the same company? Should you spin out a separate business, or can both models live under one roof? A respected professor, Costas Markides, suggests weighing these conditions before deciding. How similar are the nine building blocks of each model? Do they have room for synergy? Are there conflicts between them?

Based on your answers, you can pick from three options. Integration makes sense when the similarity is high, there is strong potential for synergy, and there is no ground for conflict. Autonomy works when both models share building blocks and have no conflicts, but synergy is unclear. Separation is wise when conflicts are likely.

These changes do not have to happen overnight. You can take a phased approach, where a project starts as a separate unit and is integrated later. The right path depends on the details of your market and your company.

Make improving a business model a job for every employee, not only senior management. Every contribution counts.

The last stage of creating a business model is managing it. The world changes quickly, so your model will go stale fast if you do not adjust and revise it in time. Your company should keep checking the market, using tools like environment analysis and brainstorming sessions for improvements. A few more pointers. Organize regular meetings with cross-functional teams to monitor opportunities and risks. Their varied backgrounds help the company prepare for uncertainty. Create a portfolio of business models, where successful ones fund the testing of new ones.

A useful note here. In the early 1990s, Continental Airlines added a new business model to its existing one and lost $140 million over two years.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
Today, business model innovation is more than a competitive edge. It is essential to stay alive. Companies should master all five stages of this process: mobilizing, understanding, designing, implementing, and managing.

At the same time, remember that innovation is not a straight line. Sometimes, the design stage reveals insights about the field, which sparks another round of design. As messy as that may look, that is how breakthroughs happen.

Try this next. Pin down your market segment. What are your strengths, and where do you shine? What will others pay you for? Sketch out a business model canvas. What resources do you have, and who can you partner with? Run an environment analysis and use the tools covered in this summary to develop innovation.

Worth reading from start to finish?
